segments.title: Constellation class shifted from conservative design to poor value proposition
segments.summary: The episode asks why a seemingly prudent off-the-shelf frigate became described as 60 percent of a Burke for 80 percent of the cost.
segments.body: The transcript immediately turns from program outcome to US naval procurement incentives, perfection-seeking, risk aversion, and requirements.

evidence.note: Opening states the 60 percent capability for 80 percent cost framing and links it to US naval procurement incentives.

segments.title: ASW ship versus multi-mission ship distinction
segments.summary: The video distinguishes FREMM as multi-mission with strong ASW from a purpose-built ASW ship.
segments.body: The FREMM comparison separates a strong multi-mission frigate from a purpose-built ASW ship. Dr. Clarke uses that distinction to question how much anti-submarine capability is inherent in the baseline design and how much depends on national requirements, sensors, and integration choices. The segment matters because it keeps the Constellation discussion from becoming a simple copy-the-parent-design argument.

evidence.note: The video distinguishes FREMM as multi-mission with strong ASW from a purpose-built ASW ship.

segments.title: VLS cost and capability pressure
segments.summary: The transcript explains why VLS and combat-system expectations can quickly raise frigate costs.
segments.body: The discussion captures one route from conservative design to expensive ship.

evidence.note: The transcript explains why VLS and combat-system expectations can quickly raise frigate costs.

segments.title: Burkeization of the frigate requirement
segments.summary: The later design section warns that Burke standards crept into the frigate program.
segments.body: The Burkeization section warns that frigate requirements can drift toward destroyer expectations. Dr. Clarke ties that drift to the critique that a smaller ship cannot deliver most of a Burke's capability if standards, systems, and risk assumptions keep expanding. The procurement problem is that a supposedly disciplined frigate can become expensive without becoming a true destroyer.

evidence.note: The later design section warns that Burke standards crept into the frigate program.

segments.title: Bridge layout shows how far the design changed
segments.summary: The comparison of bridge spaces and design stability shows the program moving away from simple FREMM reuse.
segments.body: The discussion supports the argument that the purchased design was not simply copied.

evidence.note: The comparison of bridge spaces and design stability shows the program moving away from simple FREMM reuse.

segments.title: Functional design instability delayed construction
segments.summary: The GAO-style section says functional design was needed to demonstrate design stability and delayed construction.
segments.body: The GAO-style section frames Constellation's delay as a design-stability problem. Dr. Clarke points to the need for functional design maturity before construction can sensibly proceed, which makes the issue more than ordinary schedule slippage. The procurement lesson is that an apparently conservative parent-design program can still become risky when requirements and functional design are unsettled.

evidence.note: The GAO-style section says functional design was needed to demonstrate design stability and delayed construction.

segments.title: Copying a bridge does not require buying a design
segments.summary: The conclusion notes that if only a bridge arrangement was wanted, buying the whole design was unnecessary.
segments.body: This point sharpens the critique of what the program actually bought.

evidence.note: The conclusion notes that if only a bridge arrangement was wanted, buying the whole design was unnecessary.

segments.title: Procurement incentives pushed the frigate toward destroyer cost
segments.summary: The opening explains how a prudent off-the-shelf frigate became a poor value proposition.
segments.body: This keeps the headline procurement critique as a granular chapter as well as the overview anchor.

evidence.note: The opening explains how a prudent off-the-shelf frigate became a poor value proposition.
